items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: What does voltage (potential difference) across a component represent?
items.correct: The energy transferred per unit charge passing through that component.
items.misconception: Voltage is the 'amount of electricity' flowing through the wire.
items.random_wrong: Voltage measures how many electrons are stored in a battery.
items.why: Students conflate voltage with current because both are 'something to do with electricity'; they don't distinguish 'energy per charge' from 'rate of charge flow'.

items.q: What is the main difference between an alternator and a dynamo?
items.correct: Alternator uses slip rings (AC); dynamo uses a split-ring commutator (DC)
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Alternator uses slip rings (AC); dynamo uses a split-ring commutator (DC). “Alternator is for cars only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which change does NOT increase the induced voltage in a coil?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Using fewer turns of wire. “Spinning the coil faster” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A transformer has 100 turns on the primary and 500 turns on the secondary. The primary voltage is 20 V. What is the secondary voltage, in V?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
